data.title: QBTS: With Its Quantum Leap Priced In, Jump In On A Dip
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome MW Valuations as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Thesis

D-Wave (NYSE: NYSE:QBTS) is a pioneer in commercializing quantum computing systems, specializing in quantum annealing models that increase optimization efficiency for enterprises and developers. The company is currently in the early stages of monetization with modest but growing revenues driven by sales of its Advantage quantum computing systems. Recent claims of D-Wave’s systems solving problems and completing simulations beyond the reach of the most powerful supercomputers highlighted the utmost potential for quantum computing systems.

Quantum computing sparks the beginning of an evolution in the computing ecosystem, through revolutionizing industries by solving complex problems unattainable by classic computers. From accelerating pharmaceutical discoveries to advancing artificial intelligence, quantum computing technology spans numerous industries with unlimited economic implications. D-Wave stands at the forefront, transforming experimental breakthroughs to commercial usage.

Despite strong growth forecast, D-Wave’s current share price of $15.71 is overvalued based on my intrinsic value estimate. Balancing this currently high share price with the supportive qualitative factors driving it, I equally weighted these elements with my DCF valuation, yielding a blended price target of US $9.69. Given the fundamental analysis, a forecast of revenue growth of 81% CAGR, and a discount rate of 10.93%, I derived a base 162% stock price premium. This divergence stems from the company’s pre-profit stage, reliance on capital injections, and the uncertainty around commercializing quantum systems. While D-Wave shows promise in its transition to Quantum Computing as a System (QCaaS), the current market price fails to align with the intrinsic valuation. Therefore I recommend a Hold rating based on intrinsic value misalignment.

Company Overview

Founded in 1999 and based in Palo Alto, California, D-Wave entered the scene when quantum computing was still largely unfamiliar. When investors doubled down on the internet boom, D-Wave was already betting early on quantum annealing as a practical solution for commercializing. Over time, it evolved into a pioneer in offering quantum computing systems, with offices in California and Canada. Its Leap™ cloud platform and Advantage system with 5000+ qubit marked a shift from R&D to enterprise usage. D-Wave’s introduction of its new Advantage2 system further strengthens its position in scalable, business-ready quantum solutions.

Business Model

D-Wave pursues multiple revenue streams in its go-to-market model, enabling the company to scale the adoption of its quantum systems and extend its sales reach. This model enabled D-Wave to begin monetizing hardware and recurring cloud services. Leap Quantum cloud services provides access to D-Wave’s quantum computers and solvers.

Direct Sales Strategy

The D-Wave Launch program has anchored the acceleration of quantum technology adoption. D-Wave’s team of specialists help enterprises ease system integration into their operations. By providing Quantum Computing as a Service (QCaaS), enabling customers to run the full application on an ongoing basis, D-Wave effectively generates recurring revenue.

Partner Strategy

D-Wave partners with third-party consultants and integrators such as Deloitte (privately held), and resellers such as NEC (OTCPK:NIPNF) and Strangeworks (also private). This enables the company to resell their Leap quantum cloud service globally, assisting the scalability of the business. D-Wave expands its reach by leveraging Amazon Web Service (AWS) marketplace to enable AWS customers to purchase Leap and other cloud services independently.

Developer Strategy

Through D-Wave’s Quantum LaunchPad program, new customers receive a free trial to its Leap quantum cloud service. This technical engagement through the Quantum LaunchPad program enables D-Wave to support participants with application development needs, identifying new technology adoption capabilities. This program enabled D-Wave to potentially convert participants into paid customers.

Product Offerings

D-Wave’s main product line is its cloud service (QCaaS), followed by its hardware sales segment (sales of quantum computers and hybrid solvers). D-Wave’s cloud service generates revenue from recurring subscriptions and service plans and produces a moderate margin. Hardware sales boasts an extremely high margin business, selling quantum annealing computers. By selling through strategic partners including Deloitte, NEC and uptownBasel/quantumBasel (private), D-Wave rapidly scales market reach without the delay of building B2C sales teams.

QCaaS

D-Wave’s QCaaS includes access to annealing computers and classic quantum hybrid solvers. The company debuted with its Leap cloud service between the start of 2018 and end of 2024, and cloud revenue grew at a Compounded Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of 24%, representing approximately 78% of its generated revenue. D-Wave currently possess two types of cloud revenue contracts: A large multilayer engagement and smaller multi-month recurring contract. Given things considered, the adoption of cloud-based online quantum computing platform solutions is relatively new and unproven.

Ocean Software Development Kit

D-Wave provides open-source python programming tools on the D-Wave GitHub repository and is designed to work with its quantum systems. This aims to simplify the process of building quantum hybrid applications efficiently.

Hardware Sales

D-Wave pioneers in commercializing quantum annealing computing systems, and offers scientific and technical capabilities in hardware, enabling the support of business applications at the production scale. Hardware sales has an irregular revenue stream by nature. In Q1 2025, D-Wave experienced a 509% revenue increase to $15 million as compared to Q1 2024, fueled by a commercial sale of the Advantage system. This reflects a significant expansion in its go-to market by offering on-premises system sales.

Industry Overview and Competitive Positioning

D-Wave operates in the quantum computing systems industry, a subsector within Information Technology. As per Statista, the industry is estimated to be worth around US $1.4 billion in 2024 and is estimated to grow at a 43% CAGR, to $9.066 billion in 2030. The quantum computing industry consists of several upcoming firms as well as bulge brackets, such as IBM (IBM); Google (GOOG); IonQ (IONQ); Rigetti (RGTI), among others, that offer quantum systems using different mechanics. These include quantum annealing, gate based, topological and adiabatic quantum computing.

Competitive Positioning

As a major player in quantum annealing, D-Wave holds an estimated 70% of the annealing hardware market. However, its quantum systems market share remains small at just 0.6% in FY2024, based on $8.8 million in revenue as compared to the $1.45 billion total market. In Q1 2025, D-revenue spiked 70% to $15 million, driven by annealing system hardware sales, highlighting the revenue inconsistency of high-value capital investments. As D-Wave transitions from one-off system sales to QCaaS via Leap cloud platform, a reduction in volatility and an increase in recurring revenue could be expected. This shift makes it complicated to assess the company’s true value in the market today. A present value of future cash flows could better reflect D-Wave’s long-term potential.

This however, does not mean that D-Wave is phasing out hardware sales as a revenue stream. The new release of Advantage 2 system boasts 4,400+ qubits and is available through their Leap quantum cloud service as well as on-premises purchases through hardware sales.

Pioneering Commercialization

D-Wave was the first company to commercialize quantum computing systems, releasing its first system (D-Wave One) in 2011 and selling it to majors players such as Lockheed Martin (LMT). This marked the beginning of quantum hardware market and gives D-Wave a deep installed base and industry credibility. By 2013, its 512 qubit system was also purchased by a Google-NASA-USC consortium.

Specialization in Quantum Annealing

D-Wave is one of the few firms specializing exclusively in quantum annealing that solves combinatorial optimization problems, compared to generalized gate-based systems. The 5000+ qubit advantage quantum computer has already expanded to Advantage 2 system offering 7000+ qubits, larger than any gate-based system.

Contrasting Competitors

Gate-based Peers

Publicly traded peers include IonQ, Rigetti and Quantum Computing Inc (QUBT), which focus on gate-based quantum computing — a more diverse scalable system viewed as a long-term path to universal quantum commercialization. Gate-based quantum computers are able to solve a wide range of computational problems including those beyond optimization — a feature quantum only annealers are able to perform.

IonQ currently leads this sector in monetization, with $43 million in total revenue FY2024. IonQ boasts strong partnerships with large brand names such as AWS, Azure and Google Cloud – ultimately leading to IonQ having a strong brand association. IonQ’s ability to raise significant capital and its view as a leader in commercial quantum computing has contributed to its recent success.

Rigetti, though less successful than IonQ, has developed its own superconducting gate-based system, integrated with a hybrid classical quantum computing platform. Rigetti lags slightly behind with $11 million in revenue, and faces operational and technical headwinds such as experiencing significant cash burn. Despite this, Rigetti has managed to establish research partnerships, particularly within the United States government and aerospace sectors. Rigetti’s recent restructuring that included layoffs and changes in executive leadership implies uncertainty over long term execution capabilities as well as financial sustainability.

Quantum Computing Inc. initially started off as a software-layer application business. It remains the least mature in the relative company group and is still a speculative software-first competitor with negligible revenue of $ 0.5 million. Quantum Computing Inc, recently acquired QPhoton in 2022, a developer in quantum nanophotonic technologies. This acquisition has provided a start for Quantum Computing Inc. in developing system sales.

In this group of early-stage quantum computing comparable companies, none of them currently provide hardware system sales as a product and only focuses on QCaaS cloud services. D-Wave's hardware sales throughout the years are a high-margin revenue stream. This is an advantage D-Wave has to generate high-margin, project-based hardware sales revenue. In combination with a growing QCaaS model, this enhances D-Wave’s commercial flexibility and blended margin profile. Furthermore, D-Wave has started to develop gate-based models, on top of its already available annealers and hybrid solvers, positioning D-Wave's solution offerings across the spectrum of the quantum computing market. D-Wave’s experience in developing annealers and hybrid solvers gives it an edge over its peers.

Valuation

Assumptions

Please refer to QBTS 10-K (2023-2024) and QBTS 10-K (2021-2022) for more information on its financials.

Revenue Growth

Explosive YoY growth was due to a low revenue base from the beginning of its commercial traction in 2024, where fiscal year revenue was $8.8 million. Q1 2025 has already experienced a realized revenue growth rate of 170% from 2024, driven by a sale of D-Wave’s advantage system for $15 million. I expect revenue growth to increase from both hardware sales and QCaaS, although I estimate a higher revenue contribution from QCaaS as D-Wave starts heavily monetizing its systems. As compared to similar SaaS companies, revenue tends to decrease down to 30-40% YoY growth by as the company matures. This growth trend shows a classic S-curve adoption pattern post-inflection in 2025, following by a gradual normalization in recurring revenue.

Discount Rate

I estimated a WACC of approximately 10.93% for D-Wave, using the capital asset pricing model to calculate the cost of equity. I used 4.40% as the risk free rate (the U.S. 10-year yield). I calculated D-Wave’s beta by re-levering the unlevered industry beta, and adjusting it to D-Wave’s capital structure. This returned a levered beta of 1.33. The expected market return of 10.31% was the average S&P 500’s return over the last 30 years. And so, the last 30 years was used, adjusted for the return in more relevant years. I calculated the after tax cost of debt based on weighted average interest rate incurred on D-Wave’s debt, which is approximated 2.43%.

Terminal Growth Rate

I estimated a terminal growth rate of 4.10% based on an industry average of a universe of relatively comparable company analysis, including Rigetti, IonQ, Quantum Computing Inc, etc., that had similar financials to D-Wave. This is also in par with mature SaaS companies.

Exit Multiple

Explicit EV/EBITDA multiples often don't exist for these companies due to negative EBITDA given their financial nature. Hence, I defaulted to using EV/Revenue multiples. I estimated EV/Revenue multiple at 7.70x through averaging a precedent transaction analysis of recent buyouts of SaaS and early stage tech companies. Transactions include, Thoma Bravo’s acquisition of Coupa Software, and more.

Terminal Value

I calculated two discounted terminal values in the models: a perpetual growth method and exit multiple method, yielding discounted terminal values of $570.4 million and $813.2 respectively.

Sensitivity Analysis

I modified the WACC, terminal growth rate and terminal multiple to assess the effect on D-Wave’s intrinsic valuation target price (equity value per share). Conservative independent variables are: WACC (8.93% - 12.93%), terminal growth rate (3.1% - 5.1%) and terminal multiple EV/REVENUE (6.7x-8.7x). The relationship between these variable ranges yielded a price target of ($7.09-$18.54) for the perpetuity growth method and ($7.55-$10.90) for the exit multiple method.

Scenario Analysis

I conducted a scenario analysis to evaluate the effect of different input rates and values (revenue growth rates, capital expenditure, and R&D expenditure), on the company's intrinsic valuation and its stock market premium/discount. I used three different operating scenarios for financial statement projections: Upside Scenario, Base Scenario and Downside Scenario. The different scenarios enabled the prediction of various outcomes of revenue growth, capital expenditure, and research & development. An equal blend of metrics between the 2 model types yielded stock price premiums of 136%, 162% and 211% from the best to the worst case scenarios.

Dilution

In the DCF model, I assume dilution continues at a high rate through 2027 as D-Wave remains pre-profit and is reliant on external equity funding. However, we see this to gradually decrease to 13% (average, compounded annually till 20230 of term year) when D-Wave becomes profitable and achieves positive cash flow.

Discounted Cash Flow Valuation

Methodology

I use an Unlevered DCF model to value D-Wave’s future cash flow over a Levered DCF model.

This DCF model uses an equal blend of (1) perpetuity growth method and (2) exit multiple method in the final calculation. This is due to the limited universe of relative companies with similar financials to D-Wave. Terminal multiples are most reliable when applied to matured companies and D-Wave likely would not have a clean EBITDA or EBIT multiple by 2029.

D-Wave is not profitable yet, and has inconsistent financing in regard to term loans and equity issuances. A levered DCF would include the effect of net interest expense/income and debt repayments. As the company’s capital structure is evolving (from instance, equity raises via Lincoln Park), an unlevered DCF would isolate operating performance and provide a valuation to all equity providers.

Equity value per share can be used as a representation of intrinsic value. Obtained from a discounted cash flow model, it measures a company’s share price worth based on objective fundamental factors such as free cash flow, assets, and earnings. This is a contrarian approach to a stock price, which is not only influenced by intrinsic factors, but also external factors. A stock may have a Buy rating if stock price falls below its intrinsic value and a Sell rating if stock price rises beyond its intrinsic value.

At a current stock price of $15.71, all nine modeled scenarios point to a premium relative to intrinsic value, suggesting the stock is trading above its fundamental worth. Based on solely operational drivers, D-Wave appears slightly overvalued. Even in the best-case scenario, the stock trades at a 136% premium, largely reflecting investor optimism about the future economic potential of quantum computing rather than near-term fundamentals.

Price upswings for D-Wave appears to be driven by several extrinsic factors that investors are pricing in above operational performance:

Speculative Investor Sentiment

The recent quantum computing rally was driven by the increase in recognition for potentials for breakthroughs by quantum computing. These include revolutionized drug discovery, cybersecurity and artificial intelligence.

Overall Tech Theme Bullishness

Quantum computing is often correlated with artificial intelligence, blockchain, and other disruptive technologies. Positive breakthroughs or sentiments in these peer related sectors could spill over into quantum computing, leading to an increase in stock price.

Media coverage

Positive and optimistic announcements of technological breakthroughs or partnerships could influence price momentum. Coverage of recent geopolitical moves or decisions could instill fear or greed into investors that may also impact price movement.

Catalysts

Positive

Enterprise Traction

Breakthroughs in D-Wave’s commercial annealing and QCaaS systems could significantly expand addressable market, such as an increase in AI’s efficiency reliance on quantum systems. This could also result in the heavy adoption of quantum systems (gate-based and annealing) by fortune 500 companies and government agencies.

Emerging Market Potential

With new cross-industry uses of quantum systems emerging, such as drug discovery and cybersecurity, if D-Wave successfully overcomes operational headwinds, could lead to broader enterprise adoption of quantum systems. McKinsey estimates a long term potential in TAM, for quantum computing market to grow to $80 billion by 2035.

Negative

Decoherence and Accessibility

Currently, D-Wave faces headwinds in scalability due to the complex process of connecting a large number of qubits to create larger quantum systems. Furthermore, quantum systems are fragile hardware, susceptible to noise, vibrations and thermal fluctuations. Due to the fragile state of qubits, slight vibrations could affect them significantly and cause stored information to be lost (known as decoherence). Decoherence poses a huge risk and challenge in quantum computing. These costs of maintenance would add further costs to the already expensive cost of purchasing and operating quantum systems, making it not accessible for widespread use, which might result in slow adoption of quantum systems.

Funding Dilution

D-Wave’s capital intensive phase requires high funding needs for operating expenses such as research and development. D-Wave mainly relies on equity funding. The increased share count could reduce intrinsic equity value per share even if enterprise value holds. With a projected EBITDA of negative $64 million and a negative unlevered free cash flow of US $68 million in 2025, D-Wave is still in its pre-profit stage. It currently has extremely high research and development expenses of 400% revenue. The company currently relies on frequent capital injections such as equity providers and Special Purpose Acquisition Company (SPAC) proceeds. If further funding is limited, D-Wave’s operations could face significant risk and disruption.

Risks to the Thesis

A key risk to this thesis is the potential acquisition offer by a strategic acquirer to pursue D-Wave due to potential synergies and strategic positioning — M&A. D-Wave’s stock price could rally beyond my fundamental valuation due to offer premiums from an acquirer. This would result in a stock price that does not justify intrinsic operational performance as investors bet on a deal.

Another risk is capital market disruption. Systemic tightening in funding availability for early stage tech firms due to broad market fears, such as rising interest rates or recessions fears, could severely restrict D-Wave’s ability to raise equity and fund operations, considering that it is still at a pre profit stage.

Investment Risks

Commercialisation of Quantum Annealing

D-Wave is known for specializing in quantum annealing – a method tailored for solving optimization problems, but not universal quantum computation. If the adoption of commercial quantum annealing lags behind the more generic gate based approaches, D-Wave could lose relevance in the market.

This is mitigated by the company's launch of its gate-model program that focuses on delivering commercial-ready technology at an industrial enterprise scale. D-Wave’s experience in pioneering quantum annealing systems has enabled the company to build gate-based systems in a multi-layer fabrication stack.

High Cash Burn and Funding Dependency

With a projected operating loss of $56 million and a unlevered free cash flow of $68 million in 2025, D-Wave is still in the pre-profit stage. It currently has extremely high research and development expenses of 400% revenue. It currently relies on frequent capital injections such as equity providers and Special Purpose Acquisition Company (SPAC) proceeds. If further funding is limited, D-Wave’s operations could face significant risk and disruption.

However, D-Wave’s unlevered free cash flow turns positive in 2026 — an indication that it can self-fund operations after that. Furthermore, in Q1 2025, D-Wave is sitting on more than $304 million in cash and cash equivalents. In addition, a total debt of $30.8 million brings net debt to $274 million, a strong net debt position.

Quantum Sector Hype Resulting In Compressed Valuation

In the past year, quantum computing firms such as D-Wave, IonQ and Rigetti have experienced inflated valuations from overly optimistic investor sentiment in these early stage firms. This could lead to convergence of intrinsic and market valuations, negatively affecting D-Wave’s stock price.

To address this, my use of a conservative EV/REVENUE exit multiple in this DCF model aligns D-wave with competitors' risk profiles in this regard. D-Wave’s shift to QCaaS as a main revenue stream would enable the company to experience more steady recurring revenue despite a lower gross margin.

Volatility Drag

A behavior similar to a call option, D-Wave’s market valuation relies on future success. In contrast to the Black-Scholes formula, high volatility leads to a higher discount rate (WACC) due to a higher cost of equity, and hence a lower valuation, representing volatility drag.

I addressed volatility drag by using a conservative levered beta in CAPM model to calculate cost of equity. This was averaged out from relative competitor metrics. An industry-based unlevered beta of 1.20 that was relevered to adjust for D-Wave’s capital structure that resulted in a levered beta of 1.33. Furthermore, I performed a sensitivity analysis of different WACC percentages, capturing a range how different volatilities could affect valuation. This effectively reduced valuation skew from extreme volatility assumptions and reflects valuation as a distribution, not a single value.

Valuation Impact

D-Wave’s high operating expenses such as research and development expense are a significant factor in its negative operating income. Its dependency on capital injections could be risky if QCaaS revenue and frequency of system sales do not pick up. Although volatility drag was addressed, overvaluation in the quantum sector from speculative sentiment risks an inflated multiple, promoting an inaccurate valuation.

Beneish M-Score

The Beneish M Score model determines if a company's reported earnings in its financial statements are manipulated by pulls financial data from the company’s financial statements as inputs and calculates eight financial ratios. It then weights each ratio to calculate a predictive score, such that a score of less than -2.22 would mean that D-wave did not manipulate its earnings. D-Wave in fact has a Beneish M Score of 9.12, a very good indicator of accuracy of its financial statements.

Piotroski F-Score

I used a Piotroski F-Score to assess D-Wave’s financial health by analyzing nine different metrics, categorized into the following areas: profitability, leverage, liquidity and source of funds, and operating Efficiency. A score of 1 or 0 is assigned to these metrics, whether they have fulfilled a covenant or not. A higher total score indicates a stronger financial health. D-Wave score of 6 out of 9 indicates only average strength and financial health. In this range, D-Wave is neither exceptionally strong nor particularly weak.

Environmental, Social and Governance

Environment

Energy: D-wave does not publicly report energy consumption in its 10-K. However, D-wave operates its computers in cryogenic temperatures. Coupling with the energy-intensive refrigeration systems nature of quantum computing, the lack of disclosure poses risks relating to rising operational costs. However, a research paper titled “Blockchain with Proof of Quantum Work” highlights how D-Wave’s quantum computers are energy efficient. Classical computers currently used for hashing and proof of work consumes high amounts of electricity. D-Wave’s research provides evidence that its quantum computer’s require a fraction of electricity by a factor of 1000. This could also be evidence of the potential energy efficiency of quantum computers in the future.

E-Waste: As D-Waves expands its QCaaS business model, a shift of infrastructure to centralized data centers is bound to happen. By controlling the hardware lifecycle, D-Wave would thus have an opportunity to reduce its e-waste generation. E-waste could be reduced in the long term as the company transitions to a cloud-based model. However, the current lack of formal policies dictating recycling and reclaiming of rare earth materials from decommissioned quantum systems could potentially pose a problem in waste.

Social

STEM Education and Ecosystem Access: D-Wave’s Leap quantum cloud services provide free-tier access for students and researchers across 37 countries. D-Wave supports this by publishing tutorials and solver API documentation to educate on the growing quantum computing ecosystem and streamline the feeding of inputs into quantum annealers and hybrid solvers. The company partners with Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Los Alamos National Laboratory, NASA Quantum Artificial Intelligence Laboratory and USRA to usher in D-Wave’s new technology.

Workforce Development Pioneering quantum computing, D-Wave’s recent successes and breakthroughs represent a magnet for specialized talent. The company currently employs 220 specialists across all systems, software, sales and other departments in Burnaby and Canada. To grow its US, UK and Japan exposure, D-Wave engages consultants to supplement its permanent workforce. To date, D-Wave has not reported any work stoppages and zero collective bargaining agreement, signifying a healthy and happy workforce.

Data Privacy and Security: D-Wave is subjected to several legal obligations relating to privacy and data protection globally such as GDPR and CCPA. A change in these applicable laws could increase D-Wave’s operational expenses as it modifies services and features. As D-Wave globalizes its Leap cloud services, data localization and consumer data rights would be a growing compliance management need.

Governance

Board of Directors: D-Wave has seven members possessing knowledge and experience from relevant fields, strengthening the company’s decision-making position. The Chairman of the Board, Steve West, brings experience and decades of leadership in the information technology marketplace. Steve West is currently the founder of a technology consulting firm, Emerging Company Partners LLC. 30% of the board members are women, which could be improved on.

Remuneration: D-Wave has a Compensation Committee, which discharges the responsibilities of the Board in overseeing compensation policies. It determines compensation of executive officers and senior management. This ensures objectivity and fairness in compensation, boosting accountability. This is also a signal that the early-stage D-Wave is starting to follow corporate governance norms such as major public companies listed on the NYSE.

Conclusion

D-Wave is beginning to increase the monetization of quantum annealing systems, leading to exponential growths in revenue. The company has already realized a 170% growth in revenue from $8.8 million in fiscal year revenue for 2024 as compared to a single quarter revenue of US $15 million in Q1 2025. This was driven by the sale of the companies new advantage system. I forecast that the monetization of these new advantage systems would increase rapidly throughout the years due to D-Wave being one of the only companies to sell quantum computing systems, in addition to new demand from software companies.

Currently, D-Wave’s current stock price reflects investor optimism for future quantum breakthroughs, rather than short term operating metrics, which is weighed down by high cash burn, funding dependency and high research and development expenses. The company still relies on external funding from sources such as equity lines, due to the highly negative cash flow and net income. This could dilute shareholder equity if funding persists without positive revenue inflection.

Positive stock catalysts could include breakthroughs that accelerates the adoption quantum computing systems by enterprises. Quantum computing has huge implications such as drug discovery, operational optimization efficiencies and artificial intelligence. Hence, these factors struck optimism in investors, inflating D-Wave’s stock price.

D-Wave is forecasted to experience an increase in recurring revenue over time. Despite the overvaluation, the model expects a surge in revenue from increased system and QCaaS monetization that has already been in effect in Q1 2025. A focus on QCaaS revenue streams would increase the inflow of recurring revenue, which would consistently increase operating income we see a potential in commercialization of QCaaS worldwide.

D-Wave’s current share price of ~$15.71 is slightly overvalued based on my intrinsic value estimate. This report relied on an equally weighted split of DCF valuation using the perpetuity growth method and exit multiple method, yielding a blended price target of $9.69 for my base case scenario. Given my fundamental analysis, I forecast revenue growth CAGR of 63%, in line with similar early-stage SaaS companies.

DCF outputs from the perpetuity growth method and exit multiple were equally blended. From a scenario analysis, I observe a stock price premium of 136%, 162%, and 211% for best case, base case and worst case respectively.

I recommend a HOLD based on the fundamental outlook at a blended price target of $9.69 for my base case scenario. While I recognize the substantial long-term implications and optionality from potential breakthroughs in quantum computing, near term fundamentals do not justify the overvalued stock price. While the current premium reflects market optimism around quantum computing’s future potential, I believe equity values will eventually converge and align more closely with D-Wave’s intrinsic cash flow generation as the company matures and proves out its business model.

data.title: NYLI Fiera SMID Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • U.S. equity market returns generally turned negative for the first quarter of 2025 with the S&P 500 Index falling -4.30%. However, there was a huge divergence in performance by style. For instance, the Russell 1000 Growth Index had a return of -9.97% while the Russell 1000 Value Index returned +2.14% - a spread of more than 12%. Smaller indices were weak for the most part irrespective of styles, though growth generally lagged value. The Russell 2000 Growth Index returned -11.1% while the Russell 2500 Growth Index returned -10.8%. In general, the smaller indices, delivered anywhere from a -6 to -11% for the quarter.
  • For more than a year, we have been in an earnings-driven momentum led market. Multiples have expanded and the bar for sustaining earnings growth has also gone up. Operating Leverage in the system has been strong. Ex- Energy, earnings were up 12%. The technology sector was the standout, it contributed to almost 2/3rd of the growth in earnings and more than half the market’s return in 2024. In the first quarter, the technology-heavy NASDAQ had its worst quarter in 3 years, down -10.3%. There are concerns that the Artificial Intelligence (AI) frenzy may be too stretched in the short-run and hundreds of billions of dollars pegged for data center infrastructure might be pulled back. This has led to a dramatic change in performance from the so- called Magnificent 7 cohort, many of which are down 20% (Nvidia (NVDA), Tesla (TSLA), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Meta (META)) or more from recent highs.
  • Last year, momentum and size were the biggest factors driving equity returns. Since Feb 18th, we have had one of the worst drawdowns of momentum in decades. In other words, in less than a month, stocks with the best momentum characteristics underperformed dramatically, even after exceeding earnings expectations. Clearly the high valuations and a potential change in the forward growth trajectory is having an impact.
  • On the other hand, stocks with reasonable valuations, steady and predictable earnings, modest leverage and low betas have held up reasonably well during this volatile period, dovetailing well with our approach of emphasizing stability and growth in our Funds.

Portfolio Highlights

  • Amidst a challenging equity backdrop the NYLI Fiera SMID Growth Fund (the “Fund”) returned -10.85% (Class I), and slightly underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Growth Index which returned -10.80%. Overall, stock selection had a notably positive impact on relative results while sector allocation had a net dampening effect on return comparisons.

What Helped:

  • From a sector standpoint, stock selection was positive in 6 of the 9 sectors in which the Fund was invested. The largest relative gains stemmed from stock selection in information technology and industrials, followed by both consumer staples and discretionary. Stock selection in health care and an overweight to the information technology sector which underperformed during the quarter dragged on results.
  • At a stock level, the top contributors were biopharmaceutical company SpringWorks Therapeutics (SWTX) and natural gas producer EQT Corp (EQT). Springworks drugs target rare tumors and cancers, and it was recently announced that one of its treatments for a rare pediatric disease was approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). In addition, the company has been the subject of potential acquisition discussions. EQT is one of the largest and low-cost producers of natural gas in the United States. Rising natural gas prices, a successful acquisition and improving operational efficiency have contributed to an increasing share price despite a volatile backdrop.

What Hurt:

  • From a detractor standpoint, positions in tax compliance software solutions provider Vertex Inc. (VERX) and footwear maker Deckers Outdoor (DECK) had a dampening impact during the quarter. While Vertex delivered revenue and earnings that outstripped consensus, the stock price suffered as forward guidance disappointed investors. Shares of Deckers which is home to the popular HOKA and UGG shoe brands fell on a combination of consumer spending concerns, tariff uncertainty and lower than anticipated sales guidance.

Outlook and Positioning

  • Clearly, if tariffs are enacted, at a minimum there could be a significant economic slowdown as well as higher inflation. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) could slow down to 1-1.5% and inflation could pick up by 50 - 100 bps. Depending on the outcome of the tariffs the status quo margin profile built up over decades could be under assault. The macro picture is too fluid to have a high level of conviction on the outcome at this juncture.
  • The market is pricing in a slower growth rate and not necessarily a full-blown recession. Uncertainty about the sweeping tariffs along with the unpredictable policymaking of the new administration has taken the bloom off the optimism from the end of last year. This is in sharp contrast to the assumption of a pragmatic and market friendly administration expected by investors. The market outlook is clearly clouded, and uncertainty abounds.
  • To that end, we are maintaining a diverse stance in our positioning from a security, industry and sector standpoint and continue to emphasize durable, secular growth trends to frame our stock selection. At the close of the quarter, the Fund was exposed to 9 of the 11 economic sectors and held its largest overweights to information technology and consumer discretionary and its largest underweights to health care and financials.
  • Given the environment and the potential for additional policy changes in the weeks and months ahead, we are not making rash decisions with respect to the Fund structure. In the short-run, we will look to take advantage of the volatility with the potential to enhance the quality of the Fund. Overall, we remain squarely focused on the long-term fundamentals, forward growth prospects and cash generative capacity of the businesses we own.

The opinions expressed are those of Fiera Capital as of the date of this report and are subject to change. There is no guarantee that any forecast made will come to pass. This material does not constitute investment advice and is not intended as an endorsement of any specific investment.

data.title: Manole Capital: Q2'25 Investor Newsletter
data.description:
Manole Capital Management

2nd Quarter 2025 Investor Newsletter

July 2025

Macro Thoughts:

Despite its challenges, the US economy remains the envy of the world. It is driven by resilience, innovation, and ingenuity, and we do not see anything that changes that opinion. While concerns linger, recent data points to a strong rebound in the 2nd quarter of 2025 GDP to +2.4%, a notable improvement from last quarter’s contraction. Earlier this year, we expected a healthy market backdrop, along with a resurgence in M&A and IPO activity, but the tariff situation (called “Liberation Day”) seemed to pause that momentum. In April, we wrote 5 pages on the market uncertainty stemming from President Trump’s tariff announcement, so it isn’t necessary to rehash those thoughts again.

The only certainty is uncertainty, and we expect volatility to persist for the foreseeable future. This type of environment tends to be our “sweet spot,” where we can generate alpha and outperform. This might be aided by our large position in the exchanges, or because we remain disciplined in our focus on free cash flow and durable business models. Regardless of the environment, we are expecting continued volatility.

Inflation:

Inflation remains a concern, but it is trending in the right direction. Is it at the Fed’s 2% target? No, but the last mile of a marathon is always the hardest (so we’ve been told). Geopolitical risks are certainly elevated right now, with multiple conflicts across the globe. Russia and Ukraine are still at war, and Israel has undertaken a massive initiative to remove Iran’s nuclear capabilities. These geopolitical conflicts are likely to keep oil and energy markets volatile and difficult to forecast. Last year, the Fed was projecting core inflation (their preferred measure) would be 2.5% this year, falling to 2.2% in 2026, and reaching their 2.0% target by 2027. However, this year, core inflation is running at 3.1%, 60 basis points higher. Has the FOMC median number of rate cuts changed? Not yet.

At his FOMC announcement, Fed Chairman Powell said, "Despite elevated uncertainty, the economy is in a solid position, the unemployment rate remains low, and the labor market is at or near maximum employment." Does this sound like somebody who is in a hurry to cut rates? Chairman Powell, the Fed, and central bankers are typically a very conservative group. With a dual mandate of maximum employment and stable prices (i.e., low inflation), the Fed will always choose to be prudent and rely on the data.

On June 18th, the Fed kept rates unchanged at 4.25% to 4.50% for the fourth consecutive meeting, with their last cut coming in December. It looks as if the Fed is OK to let core inflation run a bit higher than its 2% target for the near future, without lowering rates. Rather than guessing or playing economist, we rely on market expectations, best captured on the CME’s FedTool. As you can see, the market is still expecting two rate cuts by the end of this year, and we anticipate the Fed to remain data-dependent and conservative. Despite President Trump’s constant insults of Fed Chairman Powell, we envision the FOMC will choose to remain patient. The clear message has been that no immediate policy adjustments are warranted, and a wait-and-see approach remains appropriate amid ongoing uncertainty.

Despite the macro uncertainties, we remain focused on the FINTECH sector and true to our disciplined investment process. We anticipate that the Fed will lower interest rates, preserving some of its policy tools for future economic challenges. Rather than speculating on rate changes, our priority remains delivering value and generating performance for our clients.

Our philosophy is unchanged: focus on long-term growth, free cash flow, and staying prepared for the unexpected—regardless of short-term volatility. We will not chase rallies or deviate from our long-term investment strategy. History has shown that markets thrive despite a “wall of worry,” and can often use this uncertainty as fuel for growth. We believe this is the best path forward.

Conclusion:

We are halfway through the year, and the market has seen a nearly 20% decline followed by a remarkably swift rebound. While equity investors have only posted modest gains year-to-date, optimism is gradually returning. Tensions in the Middle East appear to be easing, and trade uncertainty is giving way to more formalized agreements. Inflation has moderated and now sits just above the Fed’s target, while the US economy continues to demonstrate resilience with healthy economic data. Americans remain gainfully employed and continue to spend, and companies have adapted impressively, reporting better-than-expected earnings. We remain optimistic about our ability to generate alpha on your behalf and believe market conditions are favorable for potential outperformance.

Earlier this year, Manole Capital celebrated its 10th anniversary — a milestone we couldn’t have achieved without your trust and loyalty. We remain dedicated to our FINTECH-focused vision, providing access to compelling opportunities in this dynamic and fast-evolving sector.

As always, please don’t hesitate to reach out. We value our relationship and are always here to connect.

Warren Fisher, CFA

Founder & CEO

Manole Capital Management

data.title: Semiconductors Winners And Losers At The Start Of H2 2025
data.description:
The second quarter of 2025 was certainly a volatile one for semiconductor stocks. The quarter started with a major selloff after the tariff dispute boiled over, only to be followed by an ever bigger rally. As a result, semis head into H2 2025 with momentum, even if some are not doing as well as others. However, semis may encounter resistance on the way higher, especially since the headwinds that caused problems earlier in the year have not really gone away. Why will be covered next.

Semis are on a roll, but resistance could be standing in the way

A previous article at the start of Q2 2025 was written after the conclusion of a rather weak Q1 2025 for semis. The first quarter got an early scare with the emergence of DeepSeek, which was then followed by trade tensions towards the latter part of the quarter. The U.S. government had signaled the imminent arrival of tariffs in the early part of Q2 2025, which is why the article reasoned there was likely more downside ahead for semis.

The stock market did not react well to “Liberation Day” in early April with a major selloff, and semis were not left unscathed. For instance, the PHLX Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), which tracks 30 companies in the industry, fell to a new 52-week low of $148.31 in early April. However, SOXX has recovered all losses with a huge turnaround, and it is now up 10.8% YTD, as shown in the chart above.

What’s worth noting here is that the rebound in SOXX occurred in the vicinity of a major support level. The chart shows how SOXX rallied from a low of $95.94 to a high of $267.24, and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the rally from $95.94 to $267.24 is $161.38. The significance of this is that while SOXX did at one point trade below $161.38, SOXX only managed to close below $161.38 for just one day, or April 8, to be more specific, when the stock closed at $154.86.

This suggests support held, which paved the way for the ensuing rally because SOXX did not break it by staying long enough below support, before it proceeded to turn higher. However, while support held its ground, which allowed for SOXX to follow a trendline on the way higher, it appears there is an obstacle ahead. SOXX has struggled in the last few days to move higher after closing at $238.70 on June 30, which is interesting because it is close to a price region where SOXX has struggled before.

A close-up of the price action of the past 12 months, as shown in the chart above, reveals how SOXX has tended to stall whenever it came close to $240 or so. This happened several times, including last August, September, October, and January. SOXX reached the $238-242 region a few days ago, and it appears it is once again stalling out and having difficulty getting over the hump.

This repeated stalling in roughly the same price region points to the presence of resistance close by, which SOXX will need to overcome to move higher. But the last several times SOXX got to where it is right now, the stock proceeded to turn lower. This does not necessarily have to happen again, and it is possible for SOXX to break through resistance, but this is, at the very least, something worth keeping an eye on with Q3 2025 upon us.

Who was hot and who was not

The first couple of weeks were no good, but in general, Q2 2025 was a very good quarter for semis. SOXX, for instance, gained 26.9% in Q2 2025, including 16.5% in June alone, which allowed SOXX to erase the losses from Q1 2025 to end H1 2025 up 10.8% for the year. In doing so, SOXX outperformed both the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), which gained 5.4% and 7.9%, respectively, YTD. SOXX still underperformed in the three quarters preceding Q2 2025, which is why SOXX is still down 3.2% TTM, much worse compared to the double-digit gains for SPY and QQQ during the same period.

Still, not all semis fared as well as SOXX did. Some did much better, and some lagged behind. The 30 stocks in SOXX are Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Texas Instruments (TXN), Qualcomm (QCOM), Micron (MU), Marvell (MRVL), Microchip (MCHP), Lam Research (LRCX), KLA Corp (KLAC), Applied Materials (AMAT), Intel (INTC), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR), Analog Devices (ADI), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company or TSMC (TSM), ASML (ASML), On Semiconductor/Onsemi (ON), Teradyne (TER), Entegris (ENTG), Skyworks (SWKS), Qorvo (QRVO), STMicroelectronics (STM), Universal Display Corp (OLED), Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC), MKS Instruments (MKSI), ASE Technology (ASX), Arm Holdings (ARM), United Microelectronics Corporation (UMC) and Onto Innovation (ONTO).
